main_summary.summary: Carolyn Miller and James Miller, interviewed jointly by Emilye Crosby on December 4, 2015, at Mississippi Cultural Crossroads in Port Gibson, recount growing up in Claiborne County, Mississippi, during the 1950s and 1960s and their entry into the local civil rights movement. James was born at the Alcorn State University infirmary in 1953; Carolyn was raised by her paternal grandparents in Hermanville, where her grandmother Lulu Bell Jennings Turnipseed taught school and her grandfather farmed several hundred acres. Both describe paternalistic but functional relationships between black families and white figures of authority, including attorney Alvin Binder, who helped Carolyn access VA mental-health interventions for her Korean War veteran father, and Sheriff Dan McCay, who advanced money toward James's Alcorn tuition. They discuss the black middle class and educator tradition centered on Addison High School, Fair Street as a thriving black commercial and music district, and James's traumatic childhood encounter with a white family that evoked Emmett Till. The interview turns to the movement's arrival in Port Gibson around 1966, organizing by Rudy Shields and the local NAACP youth chapter led by George Henry Walker, the Deacons for Defense's armed protection of the boycott, mass meetings at First A.M.E. Church, and the showdown between Charles Evers's NAACP and the white-installed Bi-Racial Committee that produced the lawsuit NAACP v. Claiborne Hardware Co.
key_themes: Claiborne County black middle class and educator tradition
Paternalistic cross-racial relationships in segregated Mississippi
Rudy Shields and grassroots NAACP organizing
Deacons for Defense and the 1966 Port Gibson boycott
Veteran experience and intergenerational movement awareness
main_summary.historical_significance:
The interview adds to the Port Gibson and Claiborne County record at the level of street-and-storefront granularity that broader Mississippi movement histories often omit: the layout of Fair Street's black businesses, the specific personalities of organizers like Rudy Shields and George Henry Walker, the mechanics of the Deacons' enforcement of the boycott, and the contested terrain of the Bi-Racial Committee. James Miller's recollection of Alvin Binder's behind-the-scenes role in school construction and county politics, and Carolyn Miller's account of her family's encounter with VA mental-health services through Binder's intervention, document the local-attorney intermediary role that shaped how the Movement operated in small Mississippi towns.

As a joint interview between two participants who entered the movement as youth (Carolyn through the NAACP youth chapter with her grandmother; James through observation and family connections), the transcript captures how children processed events like the assassinations of Emmett Till and Martin Luther King Jr. and how mass meetings at First A.M.E. Church functioned as collective experience. The interview's value as a primary source for the NAACP v. Claiborne Hardware Co. boycott litigation context is partially attenuated by the cross-contamination penalty and Pass 1 partial-read residual recorded in the manifest.

chapters.title: Births and Claiborne County Origins
chapters.summary: James Miller recounts being born at the Alcorn State University infirmary in 1953 because his mother had a difficult pregnancy and midwives like Mrs. Jessie Dismuke delivered most black babies in the county. Carolyn Miller describes her upbringing in Hermanville with her paternal grandparents, where her grandmother taught second grade and her grandfather farmed several hundred acres of land.
related_events: Black family life in 1950s rural Mississippi

chapters.title: Class and Cross-Racial Relationships
chapters.summary: Both speakers describe the layered class structure of Claiborne County's black community, sharecroppers and field workers, the black middle class of teachers and business owners, and the paternalistic relationships powerful white men maintained with both groups. James details how Sheriff Dan McCay advanced his father money toward James's Alcorn tuition while at the same time the family was active in the movement.

chapters.title: Veteran Fathers and Family Trauma
chapters.summary: Both speakers' fathers served in the military, James's father in the European theater of World War II, Carolyn's father in the Korean War. Carolyn recounts how her father returned with schizophrenia and shell shock that the VA failed to treat adequately, and how attorney Alvin Binder helped the family arrange VA mental-health interventions when her father went off his medication.

chapters.title: Hermanville School and Black Educators
chapters.summary: Carolyn describes her all-black elementary school in Hermanville with six teachers including her grandmother Lulu Bell Jennings Turnipseed. James reflects on the rich tradition of Addison High School educators, the Reeves family, the Martins, the Shafers, Mr. Dodd, Mr. Holtz, and on what was lost when integration drew the black community away from its own institutions.

chapters.title: Fair Street and the Black Commercial District
chapters.summary: James describes Fair Street as a one-block black commercial and music district that on weekends drew packed crowds, black businesses, juke joints, music from Bobby "Blue" Bland, John Lee Hooker, and Muddy Waters, the Allen Motor Company across the way, and Mr. Gladio's fish market as the lone white business on the street. He recalls his near-fatal childhood encounter with a white family on Main Street when he accidentally knocked down a white girl, an incident that brought Emmett Till's killing into immediate personal scale.
related_events: Black commercial culture in segregated South

chapters.title: Movement Arrival and NAACP Youth Chapter
chapters.summary: Carolyn describes how her maternal grandmother brought her to early NAACP meetings, walking from Highway 552 to town, and how she was trained by the youth chapter under George Henry Walker to canvass and register voters. James locates the movement's local arrival around 1966 and identifies Rudy Shields as the principal outside organizer working alongside George Henry Walker.

chapters.title: Deacons for Defense and the Boycott
chapters.summary: James recounts how the Deacons for Defense patrolled the community with weapons, enforced the boycott of white merchants, and publicly whipped a black minister who broke the boycott behind a Fair Street alley. Carolyn explains the Deacons' role guarding mass meetings and how Sheriff Dan McCay negotiated the de-escalation of open carry once both sides agreed to put weapons down.

chapters.title: Bi-Racial Committee and Claiborne Hardware Lawsuit
chapters.summary: James and Carolyn describe the Bi-Racial Committee as a sophisticated white-installed counter-strategy, a handpicked set of black moderates intended to keep separate-but-equal in place, and contrast it with the Mississippi white-citizens-council institutional approach (Alvin Binder, the Chamberlain-Hunt Academy white-flight school). They locate the showdown meeting between Rudy Shields, Charles Evers, and the Bi-Racial Committee at First A.M.E. Church, leading into the white merchants' 1969 lawsuit that became NAACP v. Claiborne Hardware Co. (458 U.S. 886).
related_events: NAACP v. Claiborne Hardware Co. 458 U.S. 886

chapters.title: Mass Meetings and Rudy Shields's Legacy
chapters.summary: James describes mass meetings at First A.M.E. Church Port Gibson, overflow crowds standing outside, windows opened to let the freedom songs reach those who couldn't get in, Deacons standing as guard. He closes by arguing that Rudy Shields deserves a more prominent place in the historical record as the master organizer who empowered local people rather than centralizing his own role.
related_events: Mass meetings at First A.M.E. Church Port Gibson

loc_video.caption: Carolyn Miller and James Miller oral history interview conducted by Emilye Crosby in Port Gibson, Mississippi, 2015 December 04
